This opposition is not simply conservative: it draws on a sophisticated analysis of the dynamics of cultural commodification that has substantial academic support.

Hatem takes this argument seriously without being persuaded by it. His counter-position is that the domestic and international dimensions of culinary culture are not actually separable — that the intellectual seriousness brought to Saudi food by international engagement has enriched the domestic conversation rather than deforming it, and that the self-confidence generated by international recognition has made it easier, not harder, for Saudi chefs to take creative risks grounded in local specificity. "Ten years ago," he said, "a Saudi chef who wanted to serve harees at a formal dinner needed to justify the choice. Today the choice justifies itself. That change came partly from international validation, and I am not ashamed to acknowledge it."

What the paradox ultimately reveals is that culinary identity is not a fixed object to be preserved or compromised but a dynamic process to be navigated with intelligence and intention. The Saudi culinary renaissance is genuinely new territory, and the maps being drawn by figures like Hatem, Wijdan, and Mishal are genuinely useful — not because they resolve the paradox but because they refuse to pretend it does not exist. The safety of the tradition, in the end, lies less in any particular set of rules than in the quality of the attention being paid to it.

travel_food.variant_title: Desert Innovators Lead Saudi Culinary Solutions
travel_food.variant_body:
The assumption that culinary innovation flows from resource abundance — from the densely supplied markets of Paris or Tokyo, from the biodiversity of Pacific coastlines or Alpine meadows — has always been, at best, a partial account of where new ideas actually come from. The desert, with its radical constraint and its demand for ingenuity, has historically been among the most generative environments for culinary thinking. Saudi Arabia's current moment of culinary renaissance is, in this reading, not despite the country's desert geography but substantially because of it: a tradition of extraordinary innovation under conditions of scarcity, newly equipped with the research infrastructure and international platform required to make that innovation legible to a global audience.

Chef Hatem makes this argument explicitly. His menus are, among other things, a demonstration that the culinary vocabulary developed by communities navigating the Arabian Peninsula's extreme environments is not a poverty vocabulary — a collection of survival strategies dressed up as cuisine — but a genuine innovation tradition that produced techniques, flavour combinations, and nutritional solutions whose sophistication becomes more rather than less apparent as you examine them more closely. The ancient water-management methods embedded in traditional Saudi cooking — the precise calibration of moisture in preparations designed to operate without refrigeration, the fermentation techniques that extract maximum nutrition from minimum ingredients — represent, he argues, a form of renewable ingenuity that the contemporary sustainability agenda is only beginning to appreciate.

The renewable energy parallel is one that several of Hatem's collaborators have explored. Saudi Arabia's emergence as a pioneer in solar energy development has involved a recognition that the same desert geography that created logistical challenges in the fossil fuel era constitutes a structural advantage in the renewable era. Mishal, who has followed this transition closely and sees parallels with his own field, argues that Saudi culinary heritage occupies an analogous position: "What was once read as limitation — the absence of the ingredients and traditions that European cuisine built its prestige upon — can now be read as differentiation. The desert forced a different kind of ingenuity. That ingenuity is now a competitive asset."

The carbon footprint of contemporary fine dining is a subject of increasing attention in the global culinary industry, and here too the Saudi culinary tradition offers insights that have not yet been fully monetised as intellectual capital. Heritage preparation methods that minimise fuel consumption, ingredient combinations that prioritise nutritional density without requiring long-distance supply chains, fermentation and preservation techniques that extend ingredient life without refrigeration: these are not merely historical curiosities. They are responses to a carbon mitigation challenge that the contemporary food industry is only beginning to take seriously. Hatem's kitchen operates with a significantly lower energy footprint than comparable international establishments, not because he has installed solar panels — though he has — but because the cooking philosophy he has adopted was designed from the outset for resource efficiency.

The mitigation potential of traditional Saudi culinary methods is beginning to attract research interest. A collaborative project between Hatem's kitchen and a sustainability research group at KAUST is currently developing a framework for assessing the environmental performance of heritage preparation methods against their conventional equivalents — a methodology that, if it produces the results that preliminary analysis suggests, could provide the evidence base for a genuine repositioning of traditional Saudi cuisine within global sustainable gastronomy discourse.

Wijdan's role in this collaboration is to ensure that the environmental analysis is grounded in an accurate understanding of the historical and social systems that produced the methods being assessed. "It is very easy to extract the carbon-efficiency gains from a traditional technique while ignoring the social infrastructure that made it viable," she told me. "The fermentation method works partly because it was embedded in a community practice of shared preparation and shared consumption. Remove the community and you may find that the method requires compensating inputs that eliminate the efficiency gains." The resilient aspects of traditional culinary systems are, in other words, often inseparable from the social systems that surrounded them — a complication that purely technical analysis tends to minimise.

Mishal, working at the interface between culinary heritage and international positioning, has developed a narrative framework for Saudi culinary innovation that resists both the exoticism trap and the authenticity trap: neither presenting Saudi food as charmingly different nor as aspirationally similar to European fine dining, but as a pioneer tradition in its own right, with a documented record of innovative response to genuine environmental challenges. "The pioneer framing matters," he said. "Not because it is marketing, but because it is accurate. The communities that developed these techniques were doing something genuinely difficult and genuinely creative. Calling them pioneers is not flattery. It is history."

Whether the global culinary establishment, with its deeply sedimented hierarchies of prestige, is prepared to receive Saudi cuisine in the terms that Hatem, Wijdan, and Mishal are proposing is a question whose answer will unfold over years rather than months. What is already clear is that the argument being made is a serious one, grounded in real research and real culinary achievement, and that it is finding an audience — slowly, incompletely, but with a momentum that feels genuinely different from what existed even five years ago.

travel_food.variant_title: The Silent Catastrophe of Lost Saudi Flavours
travel_food.variant_body:
There is a category of culinary loss so slow and so complete that it leaves no visible trace — no ruined kitchen to photograph, no final service to mourn, no obituary in the food press. The extinction of a flavour combination, a fermentation culture, a regional spice tradition: these vanish not in dramatic moments of rupture but through the accumulation of individual decisions that each seem trivial in isolation. A family moves from the highlands to Riyadh and finds the specific dried herb unavailable in urban markets. A grandmother decides, for the last time, that the effort of the traditional preparation outweighs the appetite of a family that has grown accustomed to different food. A farmer replaces a heritage seed variety with a commercial one that yields better and requires less attention. Each decision is rational. The cascade they produce is irreversible.

Wijdan has spent more time than most people thinking about this process, and her framing of it as a silent catastrophe — rather than a gradual evolution or an inevitable modernisation — is deliberate and carefully defended. "Evolution implies that what replaces what is lost is better adapted," she told me. "Sometimes that is true of food change. But what we are seeing in the Saudi context is often not evolution — it is substitution driven by supply chain economics and marketing, in which the replacement is not more adapted but simply more profitable to produce and distribute. Calling that evolution is a category error." The threshold she identifies — the point at which losses that seemed individually reversible become collectively irreversible — is, she argues, both closer and less visible than food policy discourse has generally assumed.

The pollinator parallel is one she returns to frequently. Just as the collapse of pollinator populations eliminates not merely the pollinators but the entire cascade of ecological relationships that depended on them, the collapse of culinary knowledge systems eliminates not merely the specific recipes but the web of agricultural practice, ecological knowledge, and social transmission that sustained them. The extinction of a specific Asir highland herb from the culinary repertoire means not merely the loss of the dishes it appeared in but the loss of the agricultural knowledge required to cultivate it, the ecological knowledge of its habitat, the social relationships built around its harvesting and processing, the medicinal knowledge associated with its use. The cascade of loss is wider than the original item.

Chef Hatem encountered this cascade directly when attempting to reconstruct a historical preparation for a heritage dinner series in AlUla. The ingredient he needed — a specific variety of date used for its particular fermentation properties rather than its flavour — was technically available: a family in Al-Ahsa still cultivated it. But the knowledge of how to process it — the specific sequence of drying, rehydrating, and partial fermentation that the preparation required — had not been transmitted beyond a single elderly practitioner whose memory of the full technique was incomplete. "I could get the ingredient," he said. "I could not get the knowledge that gave the ingredient its meaning. The threshold had already been crossed for that particular piece of the tradition."

The irreversibility of this crossing is what distinguishes culinary extinction from culinary change, and it is what gives the current moment its particular urgency. Wijdan estimates, on the basis of her fieldwork across five Saudi regions, that the number of living practitioners with full knowledge of specific traditional preparation systems is declining at a pace that gives perhaps two decades before certain knowledge systems become unreconstructable from living memory alone. After that threshold, reconstruction becomes archaeological — possible in principle, impoverished in practice. "We are in the window," she said. "After the window closes, we are in a different conversation entirely."

The policy implications of this analysis are, in Wijdan's view, more radical than existing culinary heritage initiatives have been willing to acknowledge. Documentation, however rigorous, is necessary but insufficient: it preserves information without preserving the living system that generates and transmits knowledge. What is required, she argues, is investment in the conditions under which traditional culinary practice remains economically viable for its practitioners — support for heritage agriculture, market infrastructure for heritage ingredients, consumption culture that values traditional preparation in its full complexity rather than in its commercially simplified form. "You cannot archive your way out of extinction," she told me. "At some point you have to make it worth someone's time to keep the knowledge alive in living practice."

Mishal's culinary diplomacy work represents one attempt to create that economic viability through international demand. If AlUla's heritage dining experiences, properly designed and positioned, can generate premium revenue that flows back to the agricultural and knowledge communities they depend upon, then the catastrophe curve can, in principle, be bent. It is a market-based solution to a structural problem, and its limitations are real. But in the window that remains, it may be among the more rapidly scalable interventions available. The cascade has not yet become fully irreversible. That is the one fact about this situation that permits something resembling hope.

travel_food.variant_title: Culinary Medicine's Revolutionary Saudi Promise
travel_food.variant_body:
The idea that food and medicine are not distinct categories — that the boundary between what nourishes and what heals is a relatively recent and culturally specific invention — is one of the oldest and most persistent themes in Saudi culinary heritage. The molecular complexity of the spice blends that have characterised Hijazi cuisine for centuries was not primarily aesthetic; it was therapeutic, reflecting an integrated understanding of the relationship between dietary practice and physical wellbeing that contemporary nutritional science is only beginning to develop the tools to assess. Precision in this tradition meant something different from its current scientific usage, but it was no less real: the precise calibration of spice proportions to specific physiological conditions, the precise timing of fermentation to produce specific microbial outcomes, the precise understanding of which preparation method would preserve or destroy specific bioactive compounds.

Chef Hatem's collaboration with KAUST researchers on the pharmacological properties of traditional Saudi preparation methods has produced early findings that both confirm and complicate the culinary heritage claims. Several compounds present in traditionally prepared Asir highland dishes do demonstrate the anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, or metabolic properties that the oral tradition associates with them — findings that validate the traditional knowledge in the specific sense of confirming its empirical basis. But the conditions under which these properties are most fully expressed turn out to depend on preparation variables that the traditional practice has optimised without knowing it: specific fermentation temperatures, particular sequences of spice addition, resting times that allow molecular interactions to proceed to completion. The simulation of traditional preparation in the laboratory, in other words, has revealed a sophistication that the tradition itself did not explicitly theorise.

The diagnose-and-respond logic of traditional Saudi food-as-medicine is one that Wijdan has been documenting across multiple regional traditions. The most sophisticated practitioners she has encountered — invariably elderly women with decades of family caregiving experience — demonstrate a diagnostic capacity that is genuinely clinical in its observational precision: the identification of specific physical presentations as indicating specific dietary interventions, the monitoring of response and adjustment of preparation accordingly, the integration of dietary, herbal, and social dimensions of care into a coherent treatment approach. "What they are doing is not superstition and it is not merely tradition," she told me. "It is a clinical practice that has been refined through generations of observation and that deserves to be evaluated on its own terms rather than dismissed because it was never written down in the format that biomedical research recognises."

The optimise imperative in Hatem's kitchen draws directly on these traditional clinical insights. His menus are designed not merely for flavour but for what he calls functional pleasure: the simultaneous satisfaction of aesthetic and physiological needs that he argues the best traditional Saudi preparations were designed to achieve. A well-made harees, in his analysis, is not merely comforting; it is a specifically optimised nutritional delivery system whose slow-release properties, protein content, and digestive accessibility make it functionally superior to many contemporary alternatives for specific physiological contexts. The imaging that would allow him to demonstrate this empirically — the metabolic studies, the microbiome analyses, the long-term health outcome research — is not yet available. But the preliminary evidence is sufficiently promising that KAUST has agreed to fund a more rigorous investigation.

Mishal sees in this research trajectory a positioning opportunity that extends well beyond the immediate commercial context. Saudi Arabia's claim to expertise in the food-medicine interface — grounded in centuries of documented practice, now being validated through contemporary scientific methods — is one that no other country can easily replicate. It is specific, it is substantiated, and it connects Saudi culinary heritage to one of the most rapidly growing areas of international health and wellness interest. "We are not inventing a wellness story," he told me. "We are discovering the scientific language for a wellness reality that has existed in our food culture for centuries." The distinction matters: the former is marketing, the latter is a genuine knowledge contribution that commands different levels of international respect and interest.

The revolutionary dimension of Saudi culinary medicine's promise lies less in any specific finding than in its methodological implications: the possibility of a research framework that treats traditional food knowledge as a source of hypotheses rather than merely as a body of beliefs to be validated or dismissed. If Saudi Arabia develops this framework successfully — if it builds the institutional infrastructure to support rigorous research into its own culinary heritage that is genuinely respectful of the knowledge systems it is investigating — it will have contributed something genuinely new to the global conversation about the relationship between food, culture, and health. That would be a revolution worth having.

travel_food.canonical_title: The Silent Collapse of Maya Cities
travel_food.variant_title: The Silent Collapse of Asir's Ancient Kitchens
travel_food.variant_body:
The archaeological record of Asir's culinary heritage is written not in clay tablets but in the worn interiors of stone grinding basins, in the soot patterns of communal ovens carved into mountainside dwellings, in the seed varieties stored in hand-woven containers that ethnobotanists are only now beginning to catalogue. Wijdan, who has documented the decline of highland food traditions for longer than most of her colleagues have been paying attention to the subject, draws an explicit parallel between the fate of these kitchens and the fate of vanished civilisations studied by archaeologists. "Deforestation of culinary knowledge follows the same logic as physical deforestation," she told me during a research visit to an Asir village where she has been working for the past three years. "Remove the canopy and everything beneath it dies in sequence."

The canopy, in this case, is the integrated system of ecological knowledge, agricultural practice, and social transmission that once sustained a cuisine of remarkable sophistication. The Asir highlands, with their unusual rainfall patterns and vertical biodiversity, produced food traditions unlike anything else on the Arabian Peninsula: coffee ceremonies using the whole fruit, grain preparations fermented across days with wild yeasts, meat-curing techniques adapted to altitude that bear no resemblance to the preservation methods of the coast. This diversity was not accidental. It was the product of centuries of dialogue between a community and its landscape, refined through drought and abundance, warfare and peace, isolation and trade.

That dialogue, Wijdan argues, is now decisively broken. The hierarchical structures of knowledge transmission — elder to apprentice, mother to daughter-in-law, village specialist to community — have been disrupted by urbanisation at a pace that left no time for formal documentation. What remains exists in the living memory of a generation whose average age, in the villages she studies, exceeds seventy. The archaeological evidence of what these kitchens once produced is rich; the human chain that would allow their revival is threadbare.

Chef Hatem encountered this fragility directly when he attempted to reconstruct an Asir highlands menu for an international food symposium held in AlUla. The ingredients he needed — specific sorghum varieties, a particular wild herb used as a souring agent, the dried fruit of a tree with no common name in Arabic — were either unavailable commercially or existed in quantities so small that sourcing them required three months of direct contact with individual farming families. "It was like doing archaeological excavation," he said, "except that the artefacts were still alive, barely, and had no idea of their own significance." The menu he eventually produced was acclaimed; the infrastructure required to produce it again next year is entirely unclear.

The pattern Hatem describes mirrors, with uncomfortable precision, the decline dynamics that researchers have identified in historical civilisational collapse. The hierarchy of knowledge becomes too thin to sustain complexity. Specialised roles that once distributed cognitive load across a community — the spice merchant who knew her suppliers, the farmer who maintained seed diversity, the elder who understood seasonal preservation cycles — disappear faster than any single practitioner can compensate for. What follows is not dramatic collapse but gradual simplification: more dishes relying on fewer ingredients sourced from fewer places, until the cuisine that remains is technically recognisable but ecologically and culturally shallow.

The archaeological dimension of this process is, perhaps counterintuitively, its most hopeful aspect. Mada'in Saleh and the wider AlUla region have demonstrated that physical heritage, once dismissed as irretrievably lost, can be recovered and reactivated when the right combination of institutional will and scholarly rigour is applied. Wijdan argues that culinary heritage deserves the same treatment — and that the tools of archaeology, adapted to the study of living food systems, offer genuine possibilities. Seed banks modelled on heritage conservation principles, oral history archives with the same standards applied to material artefacts, the training of culinary archaeologists who can work alongside chefs rather than in opposition to them.

Mishal, who has observed these efforts from his position advising on cultural diplomacy strategy, sees in them a commercial logic as well as a cultural one. "The global visitor who comes to AlUla is not simply looking for a meal," he said during a conversation in Riyadh. "They are looking for an encounter with civilisational depth. Asir's highland cuisine is precisely that depth — but only if we choose to treat it as such, to invest in it as infrastructure rather than decoration." The distinction he draws is important: decoration is applied after the fact, as a veneer on experiences whose content has been determined by other criteria; infrastructure shapes the experience itself, from its agricultural foundations upward.

There are, of course, counterarguments. Some scholars question whether culinary authenticity is achievable in any meaningful sense once the ecological and social conditions that produced a cuisine have fundamentally changed. The sorghum varieties Hatem sources exist today in different soil chemistry, harvested by communities with different relationships to the land than those who developed the techniques he is attempting to revive. At what point does archaeological enthusiasm become something more like gastronomic theatre — a performance of decline and recovery that serves contemporary purposes while inevitably distorting its historical referents?

Wijdan does not dismiss the question. "All revival is interpretation," she acknowledges. "But the alternative is not authentic preservation. The alternative is accelerated disappearance." The archaeological record of Asir's kitchens is rich enough, she believes, to support interpretation that is rigorous rather than merely romantic. What it cannot support is inattention. The stone grinding basins are patient. The elders, whose memories are the living complement to those stones, are not.

travel_food.variant_title: Breaking Barriers in the Saudi Culinary Pipeline
travel_food.variant_body:
The question of who gets to represent a cuisine — who becomes its public face, its narrative voice, its international ambassador — is never merely aesthetic. It is structural. And in the world of professional gastronomy, the structures that determine representation have historically been built around systems of training, credentialing, and social access that reflect the demographics of their founders rather than the breadth of the culinary knowledge they claim to celebrate. Wijdan, whose decade-long research into Saudi food cultures has taken her from Michelin-starred restaurants in Geneva to communal kitchens in Jizan, is precise about this: "The most sophisticated culinary knowledge I have encountered in my career exists in spaces that have no pipeline to professional recognition whatsoever."

The pipeline metaphor is apt and, as Wijdan notes, deliberately chosen. In any professional field, the pipeline is the sequence of institutional steps — education, apprenticeship, credentialing, mentorship, platform — that moves individuals from peripheral to central positions. The pipeline in haute cuisine has traditionally run through European culinary academies, staged in prestigious kitchens, and emerged into visibility through a small number of publications and award bodies that function as the field's definitive arbiters of quality. It is a system that has produced extraordinary technical achievement. It is also a system that has systematically underrepresented the culinary knowledge of non-European traditions, not because that knowledge is less sophisticated but because the pipeline was never designed to carry it.

Saudi Arabia's current culinary moment offers an opportunity to examine this structural problem with unusual clarity, precisely because the country's food culture is simultaneously experiencing international recognition and grappling seriously with questions of authentic representation. Chef Hatem is frequently described as a pipeline success story: trained in Europe, credentialed by European institutions, now returning that credential's authority to the project of Saudi culinary specificity. He is thoughtful about the paradox. "I needed the pipeline to reach the platform," he told me. "But the pipeline required me to internalise a hierarchy of ingredients and techniques that placed Saudi food at the periphery. Dismantling that hierarchy from the inside takes longer and is more complicated than dismantling it from the outside."

The stereotype that Saudi food — like the broader category of Middle Eastern cuisine — occupies in the international culinary imagination is not simply one of unfamiliarity. It is one of incompleteness: a collection of dishes recognisable in their broad outlines (the slow-cooked meats, the rice preparations, the dairy ferments) but assumed to lack the technical depth and conceptual elaboration that distinguish a cuisine from a collection of recipes. Hatem's career has been substantially dedicated to demonstrating the falsity of this stereotype, through menus that foreground Saudi culinary complexity not as curiosity but as intellectual achievement. The response from international critics, who have consistently noted their own surprise at the sophistication they encountered, is itself a data point about the depth of the original misperception.

Mishal approaches the same problem from a diplomatic angle. His work involves creating moments of culinary encounter in which the intersectional nature of Saudi food culture — its integration of Bedouin, Hijazi, Najdi, and coastal traditions, its dialogue with Islamic dietary philosophy, its historical position at the crossroads of Indian Ocean trade — becomes legible to audiences encountering it for the first time. "Representation is not just about who is in the kitchen," he said during a conversation in Riyadh. "It is about what the kitchen is understood to contain. We are working on both simultaneously." The systemic challenge, as he describes it, is that changing international perception requires international platforms, and access to those platforms still depends substantially on the pipeline that created the misperception in the first place.

What a more genuinely representative pipeline might look like in Saudi culinary terms is something that several institutions are now actively designing. The Red Sea culinary academy being developed in collaboration with AlUla's tourism authority has, from its inception, placed Saudi culinary archaeology at the centre of its curriculum rather than treating it as an elective or an afterthought. Mentorship programmes connecting established Saudi chefs with young practitioners from regions — Asir, Jizan, the Eastern Province — whose food traditions have been least represented in professional gastronomy are being piloted with serious institutional backing. These are systemic interventions, not merely gestures.

Wijdan, characteristically, is both supportive of these initiatives and alert to their limitations. The underrepresented knowledge she documents — women's kitchen wisdom, nomadic food practices, the culinary traditions of communities that have never had access to professional kitchens — is not necessarily the knowledge that a Saudi culinary academy, however well-intentioned, will find easiest to incorporate. Institutions, she notes, have their own gravitational fields. They tend to canonise what they can most easily teach, credential, and assess, which is not always what is most worth preserving. "A pipeline," she said with characteristic precision, "is also a filter. The question is whether the mesh is set to catch the right things."

There is no clean resolution to this tension. What exists, at this particular moment in Saudi culinary history, is an unusually productive friction between the forces of systemic change and the weight of systemic inertia. That friction is generating heat, and heat — Chef Hatem would be the first to observe — is, in the right circumstances, a condition of transformation.

travel_food.variant_title: The Science of Sudden Saudi Culinary Inspiration
travel_food.variant_body:
The story Chef Hatem tells about the dish that made his international reputation is, at first hearing, a story about accident. He had been working for weeks on a preparation centred on a heritage grain from the Asir highlands — approaching it from multiple technical angles, none of which had produced anything he found genuinely compelling. The eureka moment came not in the kitchen but during a walk in the AlUla desert at dusk, when the quality of the light on a specific rock formation triggered, unpredictably, a complete understanding of the preparation sequence he had been searching for. He returned to the kitchen and executed it without hesitation. "I had not solved the problem," he said when he told me this. "The problem had been solved without me, somewhere in the background. I just happened to be present when the solution became available."

The serendipity of this account is real, but it is, as Hatem well understands, a specific kind of serendipity — the kind that is prepared for rather than merely encountered. The subconscious processing that produced his insight had been fed, over the preceding weeks, with intensive research into heritage grain varieties, historical preparation methods, the ecological and cultural context of Asir highland cuisine, and the flavour logic of the specific ingredients he was working with. The walk in the desert was not an accident inserted into this research; it was the cognitive space in which the research could complete itself, unburdened by the active analytical engagement that had been blocking the final connections. "Serendipity does not visit empty minds," he said, with the precision of someone who has thought carefully about this.

The pattern recognition that characterises expert culinary creativity is, in this framing, not a departure from rigorous analysis but its culmination — the point at which the lateral processing of a richly stocked intuition can complete work that frontal-lobe analysis cannot do alone. Wijdan, who has documented numerous accounts of creative breakthrough in traditional Saudi culinary practitioners, notes that the pattern is remarkably consistent across very different preparation contexts: intensive engagement with the problem, a period of apparent non-engagement, and then the arrival of a solution whose completeness surprised the person who found it. "The traditional knowledge holders I work with do not generally have a theory of creativity," she told me. "But they have a practice of it, and the practice maps almost exactly onto what contemporary cognitive science describes."

The lateral thinking dimension of this practice — the capacity to bring together elements from apparently unrelated domains in ways that produce novel combinations — is what Hatem identifies as the most culturally specific element of Saudi culinary creativity. The traditional Saudi kitchen was, historically, a site of intense lateral connection: between the culinary and the medicinal, between the domestic and the commercial, between the local and the cosmopolitan. The Red Sea trading routes that brought spices from India and Southeast Asia through the Hijaz were not merely supply chains; they were, in Wijdan's analysis, lateral thinking infrastructure — systems that made available, in a single context, elements from radically different culinary traditions, and that challenged practitioners to find coherent preparations from incoherent combinations. The intuition that Saudi culinary heritage developed in this environment was, by necessity, a laterally organised one.

Mishal's culinary diplomacy events are, among other things, attempts to recreate the conditions for lateral thinking in international audiences — to juxtapose Saudi culinary elements with elements from other traditions in ways that generate genuine insight rather than merely interesting flavours. "The meal that teaches someone something they could not have anticipated is more powerful than the meal that confirms what they already expected to enjoy," he told me. "The serendipity is the point." His event design involves deliberate pattern disruption: the sequencing of courses in ways that create unexpected connections, the pairing of food with contextual information that reframes the sensory experience, the inclusion of moments of genuine cognitive surprise.

The intuition that emerges from sustained engagement with Saudi culinary heritage — the integrated understanding that cannot be fully articulated but that shapes every creative decision a practitioner makes — is, Hatem believes, the most important thing that Saudi culinary education needs to be transmitting and the thing it is currently worst equipped to transmit. "You can teach technique. You can teach history. What you cannot teach directly is the intuition that makes technique and history useful," he said. "That requires a different kind of learning — longer, less structured, more trusting of processes that look, from the outside, like not very much is happening." The serendipity, in the end, is not accidental. It is the earned outcome of the right kind of preparation.

travel_food.variant_title: When Culinary Science Meets Ancient Saudi Wisdom
travel_food.variant_body:
There is a moment in the work of any serious food researcher when the boundary between science and tradition becomes genuinely unclear — when the compound being isolated in a laboratory turns out to be the same substance that an Asir highland grandmother has been adding to her bread for four hundred years, not because she knew its chemistry but because she knew its effect. Wijdan encountered this moment early in her career, during fieldwork in a mountain village above Abha, when an elder showed her a drying rack of wild herbs whose ethnobotanical profile she had spent six months attempting to reconstruct from historical sources. "She had the answer I was looking for," Wijdan told me. "She had always had it. I had simply not thought to ask her in the right language."

The relationship between ethnobotany and formal pharmacology is, in the Saudi context, particularly rich and particularly fraught. Saudi Arabia's extraordinary botanical diversity — concentrated in the Asir highlands, the Hejaz mountains, and the coastal ecosystems of the Red Sea — supported a pre-modern medical and culinary tradition of documented sophistication. The works of medieval Hijazi scholars on medicinal plants represent an early attempt to validate indigenous knowledge through systematic observation: to move from the empirical ("this herb relieves inflammation") to the explanatory ("this is how and why it does so"). Contemporary pharmacology, armed with mass spectrometry and clinical trial infrastructure, is now completing a journey that those scholars began.

Chef Hatem encountered this continuum while developing a menu that he wanted to integrate genuine medicinal plant knowledge rather than merely decorative botanical references. His research led him into collaboration with a pharmacologist at King Abdullah University of Science and Technology, and eventually to a series of conversations with Wijdan that became the foundation of a working partnership. The compound that emerged from their joint inquiry — a volatile oil present in a specific Asir wild herb, demonstrating significant anti-inflammatory properties in preliminary laboratory analysis — had been used for generations in a fermented condiment whose recipe was threatened with extinction. "We were not discovering something new," Hatem said. "We were finding the scientific language for something that was already known."

The question of how to validate indigenous culinary and medical knowledge without appropriating it or reducing it is one that researchers in ethnobotany and pharmacology have been wrestling with for decades, and Saudi Arabia's current investment in both sectors makes the question particularly urgent here. The temptation, when a traditional preparation yields a commercially interesting compound, is to extract and standardise it — to separate the pharmacologically active element from the cultural context that produced and preserved it, and market the former while the latter continues to decline. This approach, Wijdan argues, is a form of integration that destroys what it claims to honour.

A more genuinely integrative approach, she suggests, would treat the traditional food system as the infrastructure of the pharmacological insight — would invest in the agricultural and social conditions that produce the herb, document and compensate the knowledge holders who identified its properties, and create commercial pathways that preserve the whole system rather than extracting one element from it. This is harder and slower than compound isolation. It is also, she argues, the only approach that produces durable value rather than a one-time extraction.

Mishal, who has been involved in discussions about positioning Saudi culinary heritage within global wellness tourism, sees in this integrative approach a commercial logic that transcends the ethical argument. "The global wellness traveller is not looking for a supplement," he said during a conversation in AlUla, where the Royal Commission is actively developing culinary and wellness programming. "They are looking for a system — a way of understanding the relationship between food, landscape, health, and culture that they can participate in rather than merely consume. What Saudi Arabia has, if we choose to present it in its full complexity, is exactly that system." The pharmacological validation of traditional knowledge, in this framing, is not the endpoint but one element of a larger story whose centre of gravity remains cultural.

There are scholars who question whether the cultural and scientific dimensions of traditional food knowledge can be integrated without one eventually subordinating the other — whether the logic of pharmacological research, with its drive toward standardisation and clinical validation, can remain genuinely hospitable to knowledge systems organised around different epistemological principles. Wijdan does not dismiss the concern. "Every time we validate a traditional compound, we are also implicitly validating a framework in which validation by Western science is what confers legitimacy," she acknowledged. "That is a problem worth thinking about, even as we pursue the validation itself."

What she and Hatem are building together — a documentation project centred on the Asir highlands that treats culinary knowledge, botanical knowledge, and ecological knowledge as an integrated whole — attempts to hold both the scientific and the indigenous epistemologies with equal seriousness. Whether it succeeds in doing so will depend partly on the quality of their scholarship and partly on the institutional frameworks within which that scholarship operates. What is already clear is that the wild herbs drying above Abha contain more knowledge than any single discipline is equipped to fully translate.

travel_food.variant_title: When Saudi Culinary Giants Fall Silent
travel_food.variant_body:
There is a particular kind of authority that belongs to the large and the long-lived — to the ancient date palm grove that has structured an oasis community's food calendar for five centuries, to the family of breadmakers who have maintained a single sourdough culture across twelve generations, to the itinerant spice merchant whose knowledge of supplier relationships across the Indian Ocean trade network was embedded in a family tradition that no digital directory has ever replicated. When these figures disappear — and they are disappearing, at a pace that food anthropologists are only beginning to document systematically — what is lost is not merely their individual contribution but the gravitational effect they exerted on everything around them: the ecosystem of practices, relationships, and knowledge that organised itself in response to their presence.

Wijdan calls these figures culinary megafauna — borrowing deliberately from conservation biology's terminology for large animals whose ecological role extends far beyond their individual existence. The trophic cascade that follows the loss of a megafaunal species, in which ecosystems reorganise themselves around the absence of a creature that was previously central to their functioning, offers, she argues, an accurate model for what happens when a key culinary figure disappears from a food tradition. "When the master breadmaker of a neighbourhood dies without an apprentice," she told me, "you do not simply lose the bread. You lose the reason for the neighbourhood's wheat farmers to maintain a specific grain variety, the reason for the mill to preserve a specific milling technique, the reason for twenty families to organise their weekly calendar around a specific preparation ritual. The cascade is real."

The rewilding implications of this analysis are ones that Hatem has thought about carefully. Just as ecological rewilding involves not merely reintroducing individual species but reconstructing the trophic relationships that made those species ecologically functional, culinary rewilding requires reconstructing not merely individual preparations but the food systems within which those preparations had meaning. His AlUla restaurant's heritage menu series is, in this framing, an attempt at culinary rewilding: not the replication of historical dishes as museum exhibits but the reactivation of the food system logic that generated them, creating conditions in which new megafaunal figures — chefs, farmers, knowledge holders — can occupy the ecological roles that their predecessors vacated.

The predator-prey dynamics of the contemporary food economy complicate this project in ways that do not have obvious ecological parallels. The economic forces that rendered traditional culinary megafauna unviable — the industrialisation of food production, the consolidation of distribution, the homogenisation of consumer taste — are still operating and, in many respects, more powerful than when they initially disrupted the traditional system. Rewilding into an environment where the original disruption is ongoing rather than historical requires a different strategy than rewilding into a recovered habitat. Hatem acknowledges the difficulty: "I can create the conditions in my restaurant for certain practices to be viable. I cannot change the market conditions that make those practices unviable everywhere else."

Wijdan's approach to this problem is more systemic than Hatem's necessarily individual-scale intervention allows. Her current research project, in collaboration with several Saudi agricultural institutions, is attempting to map the biodiversity of traditional Saudi food knowledge with the same rigour that conservation biologists apply to ecological biodiversity surveys. The goal is not merely documentation — though documentation is a necessary element — but the identification of the keystone knowledge holders whose continued practice is most critical to the viability of broader knowledge networks. Prioritising support for these keystone figures, she argues, provides more efficient returns than attempting to document and support all traditional knowledge equally. "In ecology," she told me, "you do not try to protect every species simultaneously. You identify the ones whose loss would be most catastrophic and focus your limited resources there."

Mishal's contribution to this strategic question comes from the demand side rather than the supply side. His work involves identifying the international culinary audiences most likely to generate sustained premium demand for the outputs of Saudi culinary megafauna — audiences whose interest is not in the familiar and accessible but in the complex and specific, who will pay prices that make heritage agricultural practice economically viable, and who will remain interested not merely in the product but in the story and the system that produced it. "The predator-prey relationship in a food economy runs through consumer preference," he said. "Change what premium consumers value and you change what it is economically viable to produce." It is, he acknowledges, a slow intervention — consumer preferences change over years and decades rather than quarters — but it is the only intervention that addresses the structural dynamics of culinary megafaunal decline rather than merely compensating for their effects.

What all three of these individuals share, beneath their different disciplinary approaches, is a conviction that the losses already sustained are not adequate grounds for acquiescence to further loss. The culinary giants that have already fallen silent cannot be recovered. Those that remain are recoverable, if the effort is applied with sufficient intelligence and urgency. That is, perhaps, all the case for optimism that the situation strictly allows — and, given the alternative, it is enough to be going on with.

travel_food.variant_title: When Culinary Civilisations Crumble
travel_food.variant_body:
There is a particular silence that descends upon a kitchen when the last practitioner of a dying cuisine sets down her ladle for the final time. It is not the silence of absence, exactly, but of complexity collapsing inward — of centuries of adaptation, of hard-won knowledge about spice ratios and fermentation times and the precise moment a harees should be lifted from the flame, folding quietly into oblivion. Wijdan, a food anthropologist who has spent the better part of two decades documenting endangered culinary traditions across the Arabian Peninsula, describes it as "the sound of overextension meeting its limit." Civilisations of taste, she argues, are no less vulnerable to collapse than civilisations of stone.

The analogy is not merely rhetorical. Across the highlands of Asir and the coastal settlements of the Red Sea, Wijdan has watched food systems that sustained communities for generations succumb to the same pressures that historians identify in the decline of great empires: inequality in access to ingredients, the overextension of supply chains that once operated on intimate local knowledge, and a growing complexity of consumer expectation that outpaces the capacity of traditional producers to respond. "When a grandmother can no longer source the dried lemon variety her mother used," Wijdan told me over coffee in Abha, "the recipe does not merely change. It begins to lose its reason for existing."

What makes the collapse of culinary civilisations particularly insidious is how gradual it appears from the inside. A dish survives. It appears on menus, is photographed for social media, is occasionally mentioned in tourism brochures. But the knowledge embedded within it — the understanding of why certain techniques exist, which local ecology produced them, how they connect a community to its landscape — has already gone. What remains is a kind of resilience theatre: the form without the substance, the shell of a tradition emptied of its animating intelligence.

Chef Hatem, whose restaurant in AlUla has earned international recognition for its commitment to archaeological gastronomy, is acutely aware of this distinction. Before each menu cycle, he spends weeks in conversation with local elders, attempting to reconstruct not merely recipes but the entire system of knowledge that generated them. "Anyone can reproduce a dish," he said during a kitchen walkthrough I attended last spring. "What I am trying to reproduce is the adaptation — the reason this ingredient was chosen over that one, the logic of a cuisine that developed in dialogue with a specific desert environment." It is, he acknowledges, an act of translation as much as cooking, and translation always involves loss.

The question of resilience, then, is not simply about preserving recipes. It is about preserving the conditions under which culinary knowledge can continue to evolve. Saudi Arabia's extraordinary biodiversity — from the date palm varieties of Al-Ahsa to the wild herbs of the Asir highlands — represents a kind of culinary infrastructure that has largely been invisible to policy, valued neither in economic statistics nor in cultural heritage frameworks. Yet it is precisely this infrastructure that makes adaptation possible. Without it, a cuisine facing external pressure has nowhere to turn, no local vocabulary with which to respond to change.

Inequality, too, plays a structural role that is rarely acknowledged in food heritage discourse. The ingredients and techniques most at risk of disappearing are overwhelmingly those associated with communities that have had the least access to the platforms — publishing, broadcasting, fine dining — through which culinary knowledge is legitimised and transmitted. The elaborate spice blends of the Hijaz, the preserved fish preparations of the Gulf coast, the fermented dairy traditions of nomadic communities: these survive in family kitchens, passed orally between women who have never been invited to write cookbooks or appear on television. Their knowledge is not less sophisticated for being undocumented. It is, however, far more vulnerable to the overextension of a single generation's memory.

Mishal, who advises Saudi cultural institutions on culinary diplomacy, frames the challenge in explicitly civilisational terms. "We speak about soft power through food," he told me, "but soft power requires a foundation. You cannot project a culinary identity you have not first chosen to preserve." His argument is that the current moment — in which Saudi cuisine is attracting genuine international curiosity for the first time, in which figures like Chef Hatem are winning awards on stages in Lyon and Tokyo — represents a narrow window of opportunity. The global appetite for Saudi gastronomy is real, but it will not wait indefinitely for Saudi institutions to build the archival and agricultural infrastructure that authentic culinary diplomacy requires.

There are reasons for cautious optimism. The AlUla culinary heritage project, in which Wijdan herself is a participant, has begun the work of systematic documentation — not of recipes alone but of the ecological and social systems that produced them. Several Saudi universities have established programmes in food anthropology that did not exist a decade ago. And a younger generation of chefs, many of them trained in Europe but deeply invested in Saudi specificity, are choosing to return and build careers grounded in local complexity rather than international homogeneity.

Whether these efforts constitute genuine resilience or merely a more sophisticated form of resilience theatre remains to be seen. Wijdan, characteristically, refuses the comfort of easy answers. "Every culinary civilisation that collapsed did so while people who loved it were still alive," she said as we parted. "Love is necessary but it is not sufficient. What is required is infrastructure — economic, educational, ecological — built deliberately, before the last practitioner sets down her ladle." The silence that follows that moment, she reminded me, is one that no amount of subsequent revival can entirely undo.

travel_food.variant_title: Healing the Saudi Culinary Tradition's Architecture
travel_food.variant_body:
The trauma of rapid modernisation is not evenly distributed, and nowhere is this more visible than in the food systems of communities that have experienced cultural and economic transformation at a pace for which no preparation was possible. Saudi Arabia's transformation over the past half century has been, by any measure, extraordinary in its speed and scope — and its food culture has absorbed the impact in ways that are only now being seriously assessed. Wijdan, whose work sits at the intersection of food anthropology and cultural psychology, is among the few researchers attempting to integrate these two registers: to understand culinary change not merely as an economic and ecological phenomenon but as a traumatic one, with the specific dynamics that trauma involves.

The concept of neuroplasticity, applied to culinary cultures rather than individual brains, offers, she argues, a useful framework for understanding both the damage and the possibility of recovery. A food culture that has experienced severe disruption — the loss of key knowledge holders, the collapse of heritage ingredient supply chains, the substitution of traditional preparation with industrial alternatives — has not lost its capacity for regeneration, any more than a traumatised brain has lost its capacity for healing. What it has lost is the specific neural pathways — the integrated networks of knowledge, relationship, and practice — that constituted its previous functionality. Neuroplasticity, in the brain, involves the formation of new pathways that compensate for lost ones, often routing around damage in ways that produce functional recovery through different means than the original architecture employed. Culinary plasticity, Wijdan suggests, works similarly.

Chef Hatem's work represents, in this framework, a form of culinary therapy — an attempt to stimulate the formation of new pathways in a knowledge system whose original pathways have been partially severed. His practice of deep collaboration with heritage knowledge holders — elderly practitioners, farming families, academic researchers — is the therapeutic relationship in which pathway formation occurs: the space in which isolated nodes of surviving knowledge can make new connections with each other and with contemporary practice. "I am not trying to restore the original architecture," he told me. "That is not possible and would not be desirable even if it were. I am trying to help something new grow that is genuinely connected to what was there before — that integrates the surviving elements rather than abandoning them."

The integrate-and-regulate dynamic of trauma recovery, familiar from clinical psychology, appears in the culinary context in specific forms that Wijdan has been documenting. Integration, in this context, involves bringing together elements of culinary heritage that have become disconnected from each other — reconnecting the farming practice with the kitchen practice, the ritual with the recipe, the ecological knowledge with the culinary technique. Regulation involves preventing the re-traumatisation of fragile knowledge systems by the commercial pressures and cultural appropriations that have historically destabilised them: creating conditions in which heritage knowledge can be shared and developed without being extracted and exploited.

The therapy that Mishal practices is diplomatic rather than clinical, but its structural logic is similar. His work involves creating international contexts in which Saudi culinary knowledge can be presented with the control over framing and narrative that prevents the re-traumatisation of cultural exposure. This means carefully designed culinary diplomacy events that communicate Saudi food culture on its own terms rather than through the filter of international expectations. It means insisting on the presence of knowledge holders — farmers, traditional practitioners, food historians — in events that might otherwise feature only professional chefs. And it means developing the capacity to say no to forms of engagement that would generate short-term visibility at the cost of long-term integrity.

The pathway between heritage culinary knowledge and contemporary Saudi food culture is not, as is sometimes assumed, a linear one in which tradition simply updates itself to meet modern conditions. The disruption has been too severe for simple updating. What is being built now is something more like a new architecture that is genuinely informed by what preceded it — that incorporates surviving structural elements rather than merely decorating a contemporary structure with heritage references. This distinction matters enormously in practice. A contemporary Saudi restaurant that uses heritage ingredients as garnishes on dishes whose fundamental architecture derives from European fine dining has not integrated its heritage. It has decorated its absence.

Hatem's menus avoid this trap through a rigorous insistence on what he calls structural authenticity: the requirement that the underlying logic of a dish — the relationships between its elements, the sequence of its preparation, the ecological and cultural reasoning behind its ingredient choices — be Saudi rather than merely its surface vocabulary. This requirement is harder to communicate to international audiences than visual or flavour authenticity, which is precisely why it is more important. "Anyone can put sand on a plate and call it desert heritage," he told me. "What is difficult is building a dish whose logic is genuinely embedded in a specific landscape and a specific tradition. That is the work. That is the healing."

travel_food.variant_title: Saudi Food Explorers Redefining Cultural Exchange
travel_food.variant_body:
The Saudi food traveller of the twenty-first century is a different figure from any predecessor that the country's culinary history has produced. Neither the Hajj pilgrim whose culinary encounters were incidental to a sacred journey, nor the merchant whose gastronomic curiosity served commercial purposes, nor the diplomat whose hospitality obligations shaped what he ate and where — today's Saudi culinary explorer is something new: a person who travels specifically for the purpose of food, who brings to that travel a sophisticated framework of culinary knowledge, and who returns not with simplified impressions but with complex integrations of what they encountered abroad with what they already know of home.

Wijdan is, in many respects, the archetype of this figure. Her research travel — to Southeast Asian spice markets, to North African fermentation traditions, to European food archives — is always structured around questions that originate in Saudi culinary heritage. She is not, in the terms that food travel media typically employs, discovering the exotic; she is placing the familiar in comparative perspective, returning always to a Saudi reference point that is sharpened rather than dissolved by exposure to other culinary systems. "I do not immerse myself in another food culture in order to escape mine," she told me. "I immerse myself in order to understand mine better. The comparison is the method."

The assimilate dynamic in her work is selective rather than total. She distinguishes carefully between elements of other culinary traditions that she integrates into her analytical framework — whose principles she incorporates because they illuminate something about Saudi food culture that she had not previously been able to see clearly — and elements that she deliberately does not integrate because they would distort rather than illuminate. "Every food tradition has wisdom that generalises and wisdom that does not," she said. "The skill is knowing which is which. Assimilation without discrimination is not cultural exchange. It is cultural replacement."

Chef Hatem's culinary education, which took him through kitchens in France, Japan, and Peru before returning to Saudi Arabia, involved a different kind of selective assimilation. The hybrid cuisine he developed upon his return was not, he insists, a fusion of Saudi with international elements but a Saudi cuisine that had been enriched by specific technical and conceptual borrowings from traditions that had solved analogous problems — the relationship between landscape and flavour, the dialogue between preservation and freshness, the design of multi-course menus that tell a coherent story — in ways from which Saudi culinary practice could learn without losing its specificity. "I did not want to make Saudi food more European," he told me. "I wanted to make it more completely itself, using every tool I had available. Some of those tools came from France. That does not make the result French."

The transnational identity that this process produced — a Saudi chef who is thoroughly Saudi in his culinary convictions while being genuinely fluent in the language of international fine dining — is, Wijdan argues, a new type that has not previously existed in Saudi culinary culture at scale. Its significance lies not merely in what individual practitioners like Hatem can achieve but in what the type, multiplied across a generation, might produce: a Saudi culinary culture that can participate fully in international discourse without having to choose between authenticity and engagement, between specificity and accessibility.

Mishal's work with the Saudi diaspora — the communities of Saudis living and working abroad whose relationship to Saudi food culture is both intimate and complicated — has given him an unusual perspective on this transnational dimension. "The diaspora experience," he told me, "is a laboratory for understanding what Saudi food culture can be when it is not surrounded by its original infrastructure. What survives? What has to be reinvented? What turns out to have been more contingent than you thought?" His observation is that the culinary identity that survives diaspora experience is typically more conscious and more articulate than what was inherited in situ — that the encounter with the absence of infrastructure forces a reckoning with what actually matters, what is non-negotiable, what cannot be substituted without fundamental loss.

The identity question that Saudi culinary culture is working through — who is it for, what does it claim, how does it position itself in relation to the international culinary discourse within which it is increasingly visible — is not one that can be answered by decree or even by institutional design. It is being answered, every day, by the accumulated decisions of practitioners like Hatem, researchers like Wijdan, and diplomats like Mishal: by what they choose to foreground and what they choose to set aside, by whom they invite into the conversation and on what terms, by how they handle the inevitable tensions between the demands of international legibility and the requirements of internal integrity. The answer that is emerging is, by any measure, more interesting than the question that preceded it.

travel_food.canonical_title: When Ancient Wisdom Meets Modern Medicine
travel_food.variant_title: When Ancient Saudi Wisdom Meets Modern Gastronomy
travel_food.variant_body:
The debate about whether traditional food knowledge and contemporary culinary science can be brought into genuine dialogue — rather than a relationship in which one subordinates the other — is not merely academic in Saudi Arabia. It is a practical challenge being worked through in real kitchens, real research collaborations, and real policy discussions, with implications that extend well beyond the dining room.

Chef Hatem describes his approach to Saudi culinary heritage as explicitly holistic. This word, so frequently evacuated of meaning by its deployment in wellness marketing, retains genuine content in his usage: it refers to the insistence that a dish cannot be understood, and certainly cannot be faithfully reconstructed, by isolating any single element from the system that produced it. The spice blend is inseparable from the agricultural practice that produced its components. The preservation technique is inseparable from the ecological conditions that made it necessary. The serving ritual is inseparable from the social structure that organised it. To abstract any one of these elements and present it as the essence of the tradition is, in Hatem's analysis, a form of misrepresentation so systematic as to constitute a clinical distortion of the original.

His position has brought him into productive tension with colleagues who work from a more evidence-based framework — chefs and food scientists who argue that rigorous documentation of what can be empirically verified is more valuable than holistic accounts that cannot be independently assessed. The tension surfaced publicly during a panel discussion at a Red Sea gastronomy summit, when Hatem argued that the scientific study of a traditional fermentation technique was incomplete without an understanding of the social and ritual context in which the fermentation occurred. His interlocutor — a food microbiologist from a Jeddah university — responded that such context, while culturally interesting, was epistemologically irrelevant to the chemical processes under examination. The audience, which included both traditional food practitioners and laboratory scientists, found itself genuinely divided.

Wijdan, who was present and who has since written about the exchange, argues that the division it revealed is real but not irresolvable. The placebo effect, she notes — that well-documented phenomenon in clinical medicine whereby the context of treatment substantially influences its efficacy — offers a partial model for understanding how ritual and social meaning might interact with the physiological properties of food. A preparation consumed in a context of trust, familiarity, and cultural coherence may genuinely produce different outcomes than the same preparation consumed in isolation. If this is so, then dismissing the social context as epistemologically irrelevant may be not merely culturally insensitive but clinically inaccurate.

The complement she proposes is not a rejection of evidence-based analysis but an expansion of what counts as evidence. The practitioner whose family has prepared a particular dish for five generations has accumulated a form of observational data that no clinical trial has yet had the duration or the contextual richness to replicate. This is not a claim for the infallibility of tradition — traditional practices include errors, persist through inertia, and can cause harm as well as benefit. It is a claim that the epistemological frameworks used to evaluate traditional food knowledge need to be sophisticated enough to distinguish between these cases, rather than dismissing the entire category as pre-scientific.

Mishal, who operates in the space where culinary heritage meets international hospitality, has developed a practical framework for navigating this tension that he describes as complementary integration. "We do not ask the traditional knowledge to prove itself by scientific standards before it earns a place at the table," he told me. "But we do not pretend that traditional practice is immune from evaluation, either. What we try to build is a conversation in which each framework informs the other — in which the scientist's findings help practitioners understand why something works, and the practitioner's knowledge helps the scientist identify what is worth studying." It is, he acknowledges, a slow process, and one that requires institutional patience that is not always available.

The question of evidence is particularly complex when applied to the relationship between food and wellbeing in the Saudi context, where Islamic dietary philosophy provides a holistic framework that integrates the physical, spiritual, and social dimensions of eating in ways that do not map neatly onto either traditional pharmacology or contemporary nutritional science. Several Saudi researchers are currently exploring the possibility of clinical frameworks capable of assessing this integration rather than reducing it to its component parts — work that is methodologically ambitious and institutionally challenging, but whose importance, if it succeeds, would extend well beyond the national context.

Hatem's menus, meanwhile, continue to serve as a form of practical argument. Each dish is a claim — precisely plated, carefully sourced, contextually annotated — that Saudi culinary wisdom is serious enough to deserve the full weight of contemporary culinary science, and contemporary culinary science interesting enough to benefit from what Saudi culinary wisdom has to offer. Whether that argument is ultimately convincing depends, as all arguments do, on the quality of its evidence and the openness of its audience.

travel_food.variant_title: Saudi Cuisine's Hidden Networks
travel_food.variant_body:
Beneath the visible landscape of Saudi food culture — the restaurants, the markets, the food media, the increasingly confident international presence — lies a subterranean network whose complexity and importance are only beginning to be understood. It is a network of relationships: between farming communities and the land they have cultivated for generations, between knowledge holders and the practices they have maintained across decades, between culinary traditions and the ecological systems that generated them. Wijdan, who has spent years attempting to map this network, draws on mycorrhizal biology as her primary conceptual model. "The visible part of a forest," she told me, "is the trees. The network that actually sustains the forest is underground." Saudi culinary culture, she argues, has a mycorrhizal dimension that is invisible to the casual observer and indispensable to everything the casual observer can see.

The mycorrhizal analogy is precise in ways that initially surprised her. Mycorrhizal networks in forest ecosystems function through symbiosis: the fungal network provides trees with water and nutrients it cannot access independently; the trees provide the fungus with sugars produced through photosynthesis. Neither organism is self-sufficient. The relationship is the infrastructure. In Saudi culinary heritage, analogous symbioses operate between entities that are conventionally treated as independent: between the chef and the farmer who maintains the heritage ingredient without which the chef's menu is impossible; between the recipe and the ecological knowledge of the landscape that produced its specific ingredient combinations; between the contemporary culinary practitioner and the historical knowledge holders whose documented and undocumented wisdom informs every creative decision.

Chef Hatem encountered the material reality of this network during the development of a menu centred on Nabataean-influenced desert cuisine. The nutrient he was seeking — a specific combination of minerals present in a traditional salt preparation from the AlUla region — was available only because a single farming family had maintained, for reasons that were entirely cultural rather than commercial, the traditional salt collection practice from which it derived. The family had not preserved this practice because they expected anyone to want the salt. They had preserved it because it was part of their identity, their relationship to a specific landscape, a specific set of ancestral practices. "I found the nutrient because the symbiosis was still alive," Hatem said. "Just barely. But alive."

The fungal dimension of this network — the element that operates underground, invisible to market logic, sustained by relationships that have no obvious economic rationale — is the component most at risk of disappearing and most difficult to replace once lost. The farming family's motivation for maintaining the salt practice was cultural and emotional, not economic; when the family's connection to the landscape and the ancestral practice weakens, as it inevitably will across another generation or two of urbanisation, the practice will end without anyone choosing to end it. The symbiosis will break, and the nutrient will become inaccessible not because anyone decided it was no longer valuable but because the network that made it accessible quietly dissolved.

Wijdan's response to this dynamic is to argue for the explicit valuation of what the mycorrhizal network provides — to make visible and economically real the value of the subterranean connections whose disruption would be catastrophic for the visible surface they support. This is methodologically challenging: the nutrient value of mycorrhizal relationships in culinary heritage is real but not easily expressed in the metrics that economic analysis typically employs. It requires, she argues, a broader conception of what culinary value consists of — one that encompasses ecological service, cultural transmission, and community identity alongside the commercial value of specific products and preparations.

Mishal's culinary diplomacy work has produced, somewhat unexpectedly, insights into the international legibility of this network logic. In his experience, the audiences most genuinely interested in Saudi food culture are precisely those who are interested in the subterranean dimensions — in the farming stories, the ecological relationships, the cultural histories — rather than merely in the surface flavours and presentations. "The visitor who wants only to eat an interesting meal is relatively easy to serve," he told me. "The visitor who wants to understand what they are eating — where it comes from, what relationships sustained it, what knowledge it embodies — is harder to serve but more valuable to the network. They become advocates, not merely customers."

The interconnect between surface and subterranean, in Saudi culinary culture as in mycorrhizal forest ecosystems, is what determines the health of the whole. Hatem's kitchen, Wijdan's research, Mishal's diplomacy: each operates at a different level of the network, and each depends on the others in ways that none of them fully controls. The symbiosis between their different forms of expertise is itself a model of the network they are trying to sustain — evidence that the underground connections which make visible achievement possible are not merely metaphorical but structural, and that the work of maintaining them is as important as any individual contribution that the network makes possible.

travel_food.variant_title: Surviving Tomorrow's Culinary Heat
travel_food.variant_body:
The heatwave that struck the Asir highlands in the summer of 2023 was, by any meteorological measure, exceptional. Temperatures reached levels that agricultural extension officers in the region had no historical precedent for advising against, and the impact on the small-scale farming operations that supply several of Chef Hatem's key heritage ingredients was severe enough that he was forced to redesign significant portions of his autumn menu at short notice. What struck him most forcefully, he told me when we discussed it months later, was not the logistical disruption but the revelatory clarity about his supply chain's mortality. "I understood in a very concrete way that what I had assumed was infrastructure was actually a very thin ecological thread," he said. "One extreme season and it broke."

The adaptation that followed — which Hatem describes as the most intellectually productive period of his career — involved a fundamental rethinking of his kitchen's relationship to the agricultural landscape it depends upon. Rather than treating ingredient sourcing as a procurement problem to be solved by finding alternative suppliers, he chose to treat it as an ecological relationship to be rebuilt on more resilient foundations. This meant investment in relationships with farming families in multiple microclimates across the Asir highlands, creating redundancy that no single heatwave could eliminate entirely. It meant working with a botanist to identify heritage crop varieties with demonstrated drought tolerance. And it meant accepting that certain dishes — those dependent on ingredients with no climate-resilient alternative — would disappear from his menus, at least temporarily, rather than be substituted with inferior replacements.

The mortality involved in this process — of dishes, of sourcing relationships, of menu identities that had been carefully constructed over years — was, Hatem acknowledges, genuinely painful. It was also, he argues, pedagogically essential. "A cuisine that cannot survive contact with the climate it is embedded in is not as local as it claims to be," he said. "Real terroir means being in a genuine relationship with an environment that changes. The adaptation is part of the authenticity."
