Wijdan, whose food anthropology research extends to the historical record of how Saudi food cultures survived previous periods of climate stress, finds in Hatem's experience a confirmation of patterns she has been documenting in the written and oral record. Pre-modern Asir cuisine was, by the evidence she has assembled, extraordinarily adaptive — built around a knowledge of seed varieties, preservation techniques, and seasonal substitution that allowed communities to maintain nutritional adequacy and culinary continuity through extended periods of drought and temperature extremity. "They had no choice but to develop that knowledge," she told me. "Climate was not an externality in their relationship to food. It was the relationship." What contemporary food systems require, she argues, is not the invention of new adaptive capacity but the recovery and modernisation of adaptive knowledge that already exists.

The urban dimension of climate adaptation in the Saudi culinary context is distinct from the agricultural one but equally important. Riyadh and Jeddah — the cities through which the majority of Saudi culinary culture is now mediated, where restaurants are opened and reviewed and closed, where food media is produced and consumed — are environments whose extreme summer heat has always shaped eating habits in ways that are only now beginning to be explicitly theorised. The tradition of avoiding heavy meals during peak heat hours, of favouring lighter fermented preparations during summer, of scheduling communal feasting around the cooler margins of the day: these are not merely cultural preferences. They are climate adaptations encoded in social practice, and their relevance to discussions of urban heat and food culture is direct.

Mishal, who has been involved in positioning Saudi hospitality within international climate-conscious tourism, is alert to the opportunity this represents. "Saudi Arabia has several thousand years of experience managing food culture in extreme heat," he said during a Jeddah conference on sustainable tourism that I attended. "That experience is directly relevant to every city in the world that is facing warming temperatures. If we choose to present it seriously — through the culinary experiences we offer international visitors, through the research we share — it positions Saudi Arabia not as a place that needs to learn from the West about climate adaptation but as a source of knowledge the world needs." The infrastructure required to deliver on this positioning — the documentation of traditional practices, the translation between traditional and contemporary frameworks, the design of culinary tourism experiences with genuine intellectual depth — is not yet fully built. But its foundations are visible.

The extreme summer of 2023 has already, in some small ways, changed what appears on Saudi menus. Certain heritage varieties have achieved new visibility because the climate crisis made their properties relevant to chefs who would otherwise not have sought them out. Specific preservation techniques — salt-curing methods, oil-preservation approaches, fermentation processes adapted to heat rather than designed to resist it — have found a new audience in kitchens trying to reduce cold-chain dependency. The threshold of crisis, as Wijdan observes, sometimes serves as the catalyst for adaptation that more comfortable conditions would not have generated. "Civilisations have always learned most from their emergencies," she said. "The question is whether we learn fast enough."

travel_food.variant_title: The Water-Food-Culture Nexus in Saudi Gastronomy
travel_food.variant_body:
There are few places in the world where the relationship between water, food, and culture is more literally visible than in the traditional agricultural landscapes of the Arabian Peninsula. The elaborate falaj systems of the Hijaz, the ancient irrigation networks of Al-Ahsa, the precise water-harvesting techniques of Asir highland farmers: these are not merely engineering achievements. They are the material record of a civilisation's negotiation with scarcity — the visible evidence of how Saudi food culture developed not despite the absence of abundant water but through a sustained, sophisticated response to that absence. Wijdan has spent years documenting this response, and her conclusion is unambiguous: "Water scarcity did not limit Saudi cuisine. It made it."

The nexus between water management and culinary development in the Saudi context is one that Chef Hatem explores in explicitly architectural terms. His kitchen's operational philosophy — its emphasis on whole-ingredient utilisation, on preparation techniques that minimise water consumption, on fermentation and drying methods that transform perishability into preservation without energy-intensive refrigeration — is, he argues, a contemporary expression of the water-efficiency imperative that shaped traditional Saudi culinary practice. "Every great traditional preparation in this cuisine is also a water-management solution," he told me. "The techniques and the ecology are inseparable. You cannot understand why the food developed the way it did without understanding the water system it was designed around."

The scarcity logic that drove traditional culinary innovation in Saudi Arabia is, in an era of global climate change, increasingly relevant far beyond its original context. The desalination technology that Saudi Arabia has pioneered — making the country the world's largest producer of desalinated water — represents one response to water scarcity. The culinary tradition that developed over millennia in response to that same scarcity represents another: a knowledge system whose efficiency insights are potentially applicable to food cultures around the world that are only now beginning to confront the water constraints that Saudi culinary practice has always assumed.

The extraction of these insights from their culinary context — their translation into a format applicable to other food systems — is a project that Wijdan approaches with characteristic methodological caution. "You cannot simply transplant a technique developed in one ecology to a different ecology and expect it to produce the same results," she told me. "The efficiency of a traditional Saudi preparation method depends on specific ingredient combinations, specific climate conditions, specific social practices of collective preparation. Decontextualised, it may produce very different results." Her research insists on the full system rather than its component techniques, precisely because the system's efficiency is often an emergent property of its elements working together rather than an intrinsic property of any individual element.

The efficiency dimension of Saudi culinary heritage is one that Mishal has been developing as an element of Saudi Arabia's international positioning on food sustainability. "The narrative that Saudi Arabia has to offer on sustainable food is not only about solar energy and advanced desalination," he told me during a conversation in Jeddah. "It is also about a culinary tradition that was built on the principle of doing more with less — that developed extraordinary flavour complexity and nutritional adequacy under resource constraints that contemporary sustainable food advocates are only now beginning to theorise." The compete here is not for resources but for authority in the global conversation about sustainable food systems — a conversation in which Saudi Arabia's culinary heritage has an underutilised contribution to make.

The challenge of communicating this contribution to international audiences is partly one of framing and partly one of evidence. The framing challenge is addressed, at least partially, by the work that Hatem, Wijdan, and Mishal are collectively doing: by the menus that make water efficiency visible as a culinary value, by the research that documents the resource performance of heritage preparation methods, by the diplomatic events that position Saudi culinary heritage within the global sustainability conversation rather than peripheral to it. The evidence challenge is more demanding: rigorous comparative data on the water and energy performance of traditional Saudi preparations versus their conventional equivalents does not yet exist at the scale required to make definitive claims, and assembling it requires institutional investment and methodological innovation that are still in early stages.

What exists, pending that evidence, is a compelling qualitative argument — one that is substantiated by the historical record, supported by preliminary research, and given embodied expression in kitchens like Hatem's. It is an argument that Saudi food culture's response to water scarcity was not a limitation imposed from outside but a creative challenge embraced from within, and that the tradition it produced is one from which a world increasingly confronting water constraints has something substantial to learn. The nexus between water and culinary culture in Saudi Arabia is not a historical curiosity. It is a contemporary resource — and one that is only beginning to be understood as such.

travel_food.variant_title: The Halal Gastronomy Renaissance
travel_food.variant_body:
The baseload of any cuisine — the foundational repertoire of techniques, ingredients, and preparations that sustains it through fashion cycles and generates the conditions for innovation — is rarely what attracts international attention. Critics write about the brilliant exceptions. Historians remember the moments of transformation. But what actually keeps a culinary tradition alive and capable of generating those exceptions and transformations is the unglamorous daily practice of its fundamentals: the bread baked every morning, the broth simmered every week, the ferment maintained across seasons. Understanding halal gastronomy as a serious global culinary force requires understanding its baseload, which is considerably more technically sophisticated than its international reputation would suggest.

Chef Hatem has made the case for this sophistication as clearly as anyone currently working in the field. His menus systematically foreground preparations whose complexity is not immediately visible — whose achievement lies not in dramatic presentation or unusual ingredient combinations but in the precision of technique applied to materials that do not announce themselves as exotic. The slow-cooked lamb preparations that appear across his menus are not, in his framing, simple dishes. They are the product of a fission of culinary knowledge — a splitting of the preparation process into its component decisions, each of which represents a considered response to a specific technical challenge — that has been refined over centuries of communal practice. "People see the simplicity of the presentation and assume the cooking is simple," he told me. "The opposite is generally true. The most sophisticated culinary thinking I know is hiding inside preparations that look effortless."

The proliferation of halal gastronomy as a global category has, paradoxically, created both opportunities and complications for the project of communicating this sophistication. On the opportunity side: halal food is now a significant commercial category in markets that were, twenty years ago, entirely unaware of its existence, and that commercial growth has generated infrastructure — distribution networks, certification systems, media attention — that provides Saudi culinary heritage with platforms it would otherwise lack. On the complication side: the commercial growth has been driven primarily by accessible, recognisable products that meet the minimum requirements of the halal certification system without attempting to represent the full depth of halal culinary tradition. The proliferation, in other words, has occurred primarily at the baseload's surface rather than its depth.

Mishal, who monitors halal gastronomy's global trajectory as part of his culinary diplomacy work, is attentive to this gap. "The certification system tells you what a product is not," he said during a conversation in Jeddah. "It tells you it does not contain prohibited substances, that it has been prepared without prohibited processes. What it does not tell you is anything about culinary quality, regional specificity, or cultural depth. We have built a containment system for one dimension of halal food — the prohibitive dimension — and left the affirmative dimension almost entirely to chance." His proposal, which he has been developing in consultation with several Gulf-based food institutions, is for a culinary provenance framework that would operate alongside existing certification to communicate the qualitative and cultural dimensions of halal gastronomy to international audiences.

The reactor of culinary innovation in the Saudi context — the place where genuine culinary development occurs — is not, as outsiders sometimes assume, in fine dining restaurants oriented toward international tourists. It is in the domestic kitchen, in the conversations between home cooks about technique and ingredient sourcing, in the informal transmission of family recipes that carry within them centuries of accumulated problem-solving. Wijdan's research has documented numerous cases in which technically sophisticated innovations — fermentation approaches, spice combinations, preservation methods — originated in domestic practice and were only subsequently adopted by professional kitchens. The direction of influence, in Saudi culinary culture, has historically run from home to restaurant rather than the reverse.

This dynamic has significant implications for how halal gastronomy's international renaissance is managed. If the innovation reactor is in domestic practice, then initiatives that focus exclusively on professional chefs and fine dining establishments are missing the primary source of culinary creativity. Wijdan advocates for documentation and support programmes that take domestic practice as seriously as professional practice — that treat the woman who has maintained a family fermentation tradition for four decades as a culinary authority equivalent to any credentialed chef. "Her knowledge is baseload knowledge," she told me. "Without it, the reactor goes cold."

The international interest in halal gastronomy shows no signs of diminishing, and Saudi Arabia's position within that interest — as the country that defines halal's religious and cultural heart — is one of genuine strategic advantage. Whether that advantage translates into a culinary renaissance that is deep as well as broad depends on choices that are being made right now, in institutions that are still being designed, by people who are still figuring out what they are trying to build. Chef Hatem, Wijdan, and Mishal represent three different angles of approach to the same fundamental question: how does a culinary tradition that has always been rich convince a world that did not know it was looking that it has exactly what it needs?

travel_food.variant_title: The Neuroscience of Saudi Culinary Genius
travel_food.variant_body:
What distinguishes great culinary thinking from competent cooking is not, in the end, technical mastery — though technical mastery is necessary. It is the quality of the creative process: the capacity to hold divergent possibilities simultaneously without premature resolution, to allow the incubation of an idea across the time required for its elements to find their right relationships, and then to recognise the moment of insight when convergent thinking can produce something genuinely new from the material that divergent exploration has assembled. Chef Hatem, when asked to describe his creative process, reaches instinctively for neuroscientific language that he has clearly been thinking about for some time.

"The divergent phase is the one that looks most like play," he told me during a long conversation in his AlUla kitchen, surrounded by the sensory complexity of a working service preparation. "I am not trying to make anything yet. I am trying to understand what is available — what ingredients exist in the landscape right now, what heritage preparations are related to what I am thinking about, what the guests who will eat this meal are likely to be bringing to the experience." This expansive attention — its refusal of premature focus — is what cognitive scientists identify as the hallmark of the divergent thinking mode: the recruitment of a widely distributed neural network rather than a focused beam of analytical attention.

The incubation phase, which follows divergent exploration and precedes the convergence of creative insight, is the aspect of his process that Hatem finds most difficult to protect in a commercial kitchen environment. "The kitchen is structured around urgency," he said. "Every moment has a task, a deadline, a deliverable. The incubation process requires the opposite: deliberate idleness, the permission to not-yet-know." His solution has been to create structural space for incubation outside the kitchen — walks in the AlUla desert landscape, conversations with Wijdan and other researchers, periods of reading in culinary history and anthropology — that serve as the cognitive environment in which connections that cannot be forced can be allowed to form.

The hemisphere distinction between the divergent, associative processing of the right hemisphere and the convergent, analytical processing of the left is one that Hatem uses carefully, aware of the popularisation-induced distortions that have made hemisphere language suspect in educated circles. His usage is more precise than the popular reduction: he is describing, not the mythology of the creative right brain, but the genuinely different processing modes that neuroscience has documented — modes that in creative expertise must be deployed in sequence and in balance, with neither allowed to dominate at the expense of the other.

Wijdan, who has documented the creative processes of several Saudi culinary heritage figures alongside her food anthropology research, notes that the highest-functioning traditional practitioners she has encountered demonstrate a sophisticated intuitive management of these processing modes — an ability to move between expansive attention and focused execution that has never been formalised but is, she argues, precisely the capacity that culinary education at every level should be trying to develop. "The grandmother who maintains a heritage fermentation culture is doing something cognitively demanding," she told me. "She is managing a living system that requires both routine attention and adaptive response. The routine is analytical; the adaptation is creative. She has developed both without ever taking a course in either."

The default mode network — the neural system that activates during apparently unfocused states and is now understood to be the neurological substrate of creative incubation — is, in this framing, not merely a neuroscientific curiosity but a practical tool that professional culinary creativity depends upon and that culinary institutions should be actively cultivating rather than inadvertently suppressing. The kitchen culture that eliminates idle time in the name of productivity may be, paradoxically, eliminating the neurological conditions under which its most important creative outputs are generated.

Mishal approaches the question of culinary creativity from the perspective of cultural diplomacy, where the challenge is not the generation of creative insight but its communication across cultural contexts that may not share the frameworks required to receive it. The insight that Hatem has developed about Saudi culinary heritage — the specific convergent understanding of how heritage ingredients, traditional techniques, and contemporary fine dining can be brought into genuinely productive dialogue — must, in Mishal's work, be translated into experiences that can create analogous insight in audiences with very different starting points. "The genius is in the original convergence," he told me. "The diplomacy is in the re-convergence — in creating the conditions under which someone else can arrive at the same understanding through their own process." It is, he acknowledges, a form of creative work in its own right, and one that requires its own cycles of divergence, incubation, and insight.

travel_food.variant_title: The Saudi Culinary Water Revolution
travel_food.variant_body:
The circular economy of a well-designed traditional Saudi kitchen is a thing of remarkable elegance — not the elegance of simplicity but the elegance of complex systems that have found their optimal configuration through generations of iterative refinement. Nothing of nutritional value is discarded; the by-products of one preparation become the inputs of the next; the fermentation culture that sours this week's bread will flavour next month's preserved vegetable; the fat rendered from one cooking process lubricates the pan for the next and flavours the dough that follows. Wijdan, who has documented these circular flows in traditional Asir and Hijazi kitchens with the same rigour that industrial ecologists apply to manufacturing systems, describes the result as a closed-loop nutrition system that achieves resource efficiency ratios that contemporary sustainable food design struggles to approach.

The innovation embedded in this circularity is not immediately visible to observers trained in the linear logic of contemporary food production — the logic in which ingredients arrive from external sources, are transformed into dishes, and generate waste that exits the system. The circular logic of the traditional Saudi kitchen operates on different principles: the minimisation of exits (both waste and energy expenditure) through the design of preparation sequences in which each step captures the value generated by the previous one, and in which apparent waste is systematically recovered and redirected. "It is not that they were trying to be sustainable," Wijdan told me. "It is that sustainability was encoded in the economic and ecological conditions they were operating under. They could not afford exits. So they designed systems without them."

Chef Hatem's current kitchen is, in its operational design, his most sustained attempt to recover and modernise this circular logic. The infrastructure he has built — the composting systems, the fermentation programmes, the heritage cultivar growing plots that process kitchen organic waste and produce the ingredients that return to the kitchen — is an adaptation of traditional circular principles to a contemporary fine dining context that involves different scales, different technologies, and different regulatory requirements than the traditional kitchen assumed. The footprint of his kitchen — its net environmental impact — is, by the measures he applies, significantly lower than that of comparable establishments, not because of any single innovation but because of the integrated circular design that no single component can produce independently.

The recover dimension of his practice extends to culinary knowledge as well as material resources. Every menu cycle involves an attempt to recover preparation knowledge that has been allowed to lapse — to find the practitioners who still possess it, to document it, to integrate it into a contemporary context in ways that keep it viable rather than merely archival. "Knowledge is as recoverable as materials," he told me, "but only within a window. After the window closes, recovery becomes reconstruction — something different, with different costs and different losses." His urgency about documentation is inseparable from his understanding of circularity: in a genuinely circular knowledge system, nothing should be allowed to exit the loop entirely.

Mishal's approach to positioning Saudi culinary heritage within the global circular economy conversation has involved developing what he calls provenance narratives — accounts of specific dishes or preparation traditions that trace the full circular logic of their production, from the heritage seed variety in the heritage agricultural system through the preparation sequence in the kitchen to the community practices of consumption and sharing that complete the circle. "Sustainability is not a technical property of a dish," he told me. "It is a relationship between a dish and the full system that produces and consumes it. The provenance narrative makes that relationship visible." His culinary diplomacy events are designed around these narratives in ways that have proven considerably more engaging for international audiences than conventional sustainability messaging.

The optimise imperative in sustainable Saudi culinary design is one that Wijdan approaches from a research perspective that is both admiring and analytically rigorous. Her documentation of traditional circular kitchen systems is not hagiographic — she acknowledges the constraints under which they operated, the nutritional limitations that resource scarcity imposed, the aspects of traditional practice that were not optimal in any meaningful sense but were simply the best available response to conditions that no longer obtain. "The goal," she said, "is not to replicate the traditional system in its entirety. It is to understand what it optimised for, and to ask whether those optimisations are transferable to a contemporary context that has different constraints and different capabilities." Some are; some are not. The distinction requires precisely the kind of rigorous comparative analysis that Saudi culinary heritage institutions are only now beginning to develop the capacity for.

What the integrate vision of Saudi culinary sustainability ultimately proposes is not a choice between tradition and modernity but a design practice that takes both seriously — that is as rigorous about recovering and applying traditional knowledge as it is about developing and deploying contemporary technique. The circular logic of the traditional Saudi kitchen is not nostalgia. It is intellectual capital, unevenly documented and incompletely understood, whose full value will only become clear when the research and institutional infrastructure required to realise it is finally in place.
